turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion of the court this morning in case 18-302, Iancu v. Brunetti.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: The question in this case is whether a particular provision of the Federal Trademark Statute violates the First Amendment. A trademark for anyone who doesn't know is a brand name or a symbol or a saying or something of that kind that identifies a product. And if the trademark is registered with the US Patent and Trademark Office, which I will call the PTO, for short, then the holder of the trademark gets certain legal benefits. But the trademark 'statute' prohibits the PTO from registering certain kinds of trademarks. One of those prohibitions is what is at issue in this case. The statute says that the PTO cannot register any immoral or scandalous trademarks. Today, we hold that provision to violate the First Amendment. The most fundamental principle of our free speech law is that the government cannot penalize or disfavor or discriminate against expression based on the ideas and the viewpoints that it conveys. The ban on immoral or scandalous trademarks does just that. Trademarks are a kind of expression and the statute's viewpoint discrimination is apparent from its very language. What does immoral mean? The dictionaries say, and you know this even without the dictionaries, that immoral means inconsistent with rectitude, purity or good morals, violating principles of morality and how about scandalous? Not really much different. According to the dictionary, something is scandalous when it gives offense to the conscience or moral feelings or when it is disgraceful or disreputable. So the immoral or scandalous bar allows trademark registration when a marks message conforms with society since of decency and morality, but it prohibits registration of any mark expressing a contrary view when it goes against that societal sense of morality and rectitude. That is exactly the kind of discrimination against ideas that the First Amendment prohibits. Now, if that all sounds a little abstract, here are some examples that may make the point. The PTO rejected as immoral or scandalous trademarks for marijuana cola and you can't spell healthcare without T-H-C because the PTO said they glamorize drug use. But at the same time the office allowed trademarks with sayings like Say No to Drugs and Dare to Resist Drugs, in other words, marks that express an antidrug message. The office has similarly rejected immoral or scandalous views about among other things, religion and terrorism, while approving marks expressing more accepted views on those same topics. The rejected marks expressed opinions that are offensive to many people in our society but the First Amendment does not allow the government to discriminate against views just because many people; whether rightly or wrongly, view them as offensive. In an effort to save this statute, the government asks us to read it to mean something different from what it actually says. According to the government, we should read the statutes to cover only trademarks that are lewd, sexually explicit or profane. But although we try to interpret statutes to avoid constitutional problems, we cannot simply re-write Congress' words and the statute does not draw the line at lewd, sexually explicit or profane marks. The statute may include those marks but it also includes the universe of marks that convey immoral or scandalous ideas and viewpoints. And because that's so, the First Amendment requires that we strike the statute down. Justice Alito has filed a concurring opinion. The Chief Justice has filed an opinion concurring in part and dissenting in part. Justice Breyer has filed an opinion concurring in part and dissenting in part. Justice Sotomayor has filed an opinion concurring in part and dissenting in part in which Justice Breyer joins.
